1.synthesis: Isaiah 25 opens with a thanksgiving hymn responding to the cosmic judgment of chapter 24: “O LORD, you are my God; I will exalt you; I will praise your name, for you have done wonderful things, plans formed of old, faithful and sure” (25:1). The praise is personal (eli, my God) and rooted in the reliability of God’s ancient plans: what has now been done was not improvised but was the execution of counsel formed long ago. The content of the praise: “For you have made the city a heap, the fortified city a ruin; the foreigners’ palace is a city no more; it will never be rebuilt” (25:2). The destroyed city represents the urban civilization of human pride brought to nothing. Therefore strong peoples will glorify you; cities of ruthless nations will fear you (25:3). YHWH has been a stronghold to the poor, a stronghold to the needy in distress, a shelter from the storm and a shade from the heat; for the breath of the ruthless is like a storm against a wall (25:4). He stills the noise of foreigners; as heat by the shadow of a cloud, so the song of the ruthless is put down (25:5).
1.historical_context: The thanksgiving hymn of Isaiah 25:1–5 is a communal response to the nation-oracle judgment series (chs. 13–23) and the cosmic judgment of ch. 24. The “plans formed of old, faithful and sure” (25:1) is the Isaianic theology of divine counsel: what happens in history is not reactive but is the execution of YHWH’s eternal purpose (14:24–27; 46:10). The stronghold for the poor and needy is the social corollary of the cosmic judgment: YHWH’s destruction of the ruthless city is simultaneously the protection of those the ruthless oppressed. The storm-and-wall image (v. 4) describes the ruthless as a destructive force that YHWH neutralizes.
1.literary_structure: The hymn moves from praise to YHWH (v. 1) through the content of the praise (v. 2: the city destroyed) through the consequence (v. 3: strong peoples glorify, ruthless cities fear) through the pastoral application (vv. 4–5: YHWH is shelter and stronghold for the poor against the ruthless). The structure is typical of the thanksgiving hymn: declaration of praise → reason for praise (the act described) → its effect (nations acknowledge) → its pastoral meaning (YHWH protects the vulnerable).
1.theological_application: The announcement that YHWH has been a stronghold to the poor and needy (25:4) establishes the pastoral dimension of the apocalyptic: the cosmic judgment is not merely geopolitical reshuffling but the specific protection of those who have been oppressed by the ruthless. Communities shaped by this passage understand that the eschatological hope of divine judgment is most immediately felt by the poor and vulnerable, for whom the downfall of the ruthless is the arrival of their protection.
1.christology: YHWH as the stronghold and shelter of the poor against the ruthless (25:4–5) is fulfilled in Christ, who identified himself with the poor and became the shelter of those the powerful oppressed: “Blessed are you who are poor, for yours is the kingdom of God” (Luke 6:20). The stilling of the ruthless’ noise (v. 5) is the pattern of Gethsemane and the cross: the noise of the mob and the powerful is stilled not by superior force but by the quiet endurance of the one who bears all things. The faithful and sure plans formed of old (25:1) are the eternal purpose accomplished in Christ (Eph 1:11).

6.pericope_label: The Lord's Feast for All Nations
6.synthesis: Isaiah 25:6–12 contains one of the most magnificent eschatological visions in the entire Bible: the feast on the mountain of YHWH for all peoples. “On this mountain the LORD of hosts will make for all peoples a feast of rich food, a feast of well-aged wine, of rich food full of marrow, of aged wine well refined” (25:6). The feast is universal (“all peoples”), is the best of all things (well-aged, full of marrow, well refined), and takes place on the mountain of YHWH. The destruction that accompanies the feast is the destruction of the shroud: “And he will swallow up on this mountain the covering that is cast over all peoples, the veil that is spread over all nations” (25:7). The veil over the nations is the darkness of death and ignorance; on YHWH’s mountain it is removed. Then the climactic declaration: “He will swallow up death forever; and the Lord GOD will wipe away tears from all faces, and the reproach of his people he will take away from all the earth, for the LORD has spoken” (25:8). Death is swallowed; tears are wiped; reproach is removed. The people’s response: “It will be said on that day, ‘Behold, this is our God; we have waited for him, that he might save us. This is the LORD; we have waited for him; let us be glad and rejoice in his salvation’” (25:9). Moab, the representative of proud resistance, is trampled down as straw is trampled in a dunghill (25:10).
6.historical_context: The feast on the mountain for all peoples is the eschatological fulfillment of the Sinai theophany (where the elders of Israel ate and drank before YHWH, Exod 24:11) extended to all nations. The swallowing of death (billa hammavet lentsach, swallowed death forever, 25:8) is the most direct Old Testament statement of the abolition of death—stronger than the Psalter’s hope for rescue from Sheol because it announces the permanent abolition of death itself rather than the rescue of an individual. Paul quotes this verse in his resurrection argument: “Death is swallowed up in victory” (1 Cor 15:54). Revelation 7:17 and 21:4 quote the wiping of tears directly.
6.literary_structure: The unit moves from the feast (vv. 6–7) through the abolition of death and tears (v. 8) through the communal response of joy (v. 9) through the hand of YHWH resting on the mountain (v. 10a) through the fate of Moab as representative of the proud (vv. 10b–12). The feast and the trampling of Moab are two sides of the same eschatological act: the vindication of the humble is the trampling of the proud.
6.theological_application: The swallowing of death forever and the wiping of tears from all faces (25:8) is the eschatological hope that defines the church’s posture toward all suffering in the present age: every tear is already theologically located within a larger narrative that ends with its removal. The feast for all peoples on the mountain models the radical hospitality of the kingdom: the best food and wine, universally available, on YHWH’s own mountain. The communal response of v. 9—“we have waited for him; let us be glad”—models the patience and joy that belongs to those who live in expectation of the eschatological feast.
6.christology: “He will swallow up death forever” (25:8) is the text Paul quotes to interpret the resurrection of Christ: “Then shall come to pass the saying that is written: ‘Death is swallowed up in victory. O death, where is your victory? O death, where is your sting?’” (1 Cor 15:54–55). The feast for all peoples becomes the marriage supper of the Lamb (Rev 19:9); the wiping of tears from all faces (25:8) is quoted in Revelation 7:17 and 21:4. The one who swallows death is Christ, “who abolished death and brought life and immortality to light through the gospel” (2 Tim 1:10).
